author: @thesentientneuron6550
text: Happy Birthday Rob! (I'll admit, I only found out about it from the other commenters). I really loved the point you made in this video which seemed intended at young people like me in the city who've not taken the time to go out into the world and see where they come from, how they're made. Go to the shops, talk to the folk there and watch how they practice their craft, ask them questions, drive past the farms/mines/whatever it first comes from, then by the first factory in the line that transforms that into something more useful and familiar. It feels quite amazing. Realizing it now, it's an absolutely essential thing to experience for any tinkerer, as I hope to be :)

author: @Istandby666
text:
As for a place to live. I only need a place to sleep, a place to cook, and a place to take a shower/ toilet. I don't need a fancy stick and mortar building. Even a tiny house is too big for me.
I've been simplifying my life since 2014. I no longer have the house or Mercedes. I no longer play the cat and mouse game. I live more for myself and thanks to solar panels, wind turbines, and water collecting. I've been able to live my life almost without money.
I'm trying to make my life recession proof...lol

author: @MACLADILLY

The roof timbers are from the nearby woods. That around 1850 ish. Local vernacular technology and materials..

author: @davidblyth5495
text:
Very good insight into technology.
During a technology management course, I commented that the "rudimentary" elements I saw in the bow, arrow and quiver system of the Bushmen in the Namib Desert was elegant technology which harnessed the biochemistry of plants to immobilise the animals they hunted, the natural features of the gut from their prey, and several other natural resources. The gut served not only as a bow string but as a rope to turn a stick when rubbing it against another piece of wood when starting a fire.
Rias Van Wyk offers the following definition of technology:  "Technology is a created capability: it is manifested in the purpose of which is to augment human skill".
He further explains the key concepts associated with the definition:
a.	Created:  Technology is the product of deliberate action.  It has to be cultivated and nurtured if it is to be harnessed as a resource.
b.	Capability:  In this context technology gives rise to the ability to manipulate aspects of the physical world.
c.	Artefacts:  Artefacts are the repositories of capability and refer to devices, tools, instruments or machines.
d.	Augment:  In this context augment is intended to refer to both the enhancement of human competence by means of adding artefacts to support human activities and the replacement of human ability by substitution with artefacts.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that is very succinctly put mate - thank you for posting that

author: @TimeSurfer206
text:
Just a few days ago, someone asked me what I would do if I was dumped on a desert island.
My response was, "Start finding rocks a bit bigger than my fist and slapping them together. Once I get a sharp edge, I make a club and some string, and then I turn them into an axe.
"Now, I need to find shelter."
